case_ch: In September 2026, the school settled the labor service fees of three high-level experts during an academic exchange week together in the same batch. Prof. Xia, a national-level second-tier professional-technical post personnel, both delivered 4 class hours of lecturing and participated in 2 days of in-person consultation; the lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis, with a lecturing rate of RMB 1,200 per class hour and an in-person consultation fee of RMB 700 per day. Academician Shao, arriving at the school in the same batch, likewise completed 4 class hours of lecturing and 2 days of in-person consultation, with the same lecturing rate of RMB 1,200 per class hour and a consultation fee of RMB 700 per day, but the lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis. Prof. Mu, corresponding to bureau-director-level and above management personnel, also completed 4 class hours of lecturing and 2 days of in-person consultation; the lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at RMB 1,200 per class hour, and the consultation fee was RMB 1,000 per day. The entire batch of materials covered only lecture fees and expert consulting fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group continued to submit the travel materials of three teachers who had traveled to Beijing for phase-based project tasks together for review. Prof. Cheng, Prof. Xie, and Prof. Dai all charged expenses to research project funds, none of the trips were made by the unit head personally, all traveled by first-class train seating (round trip), each stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of accommodation, all claimed a meal subsidy of RMB 300, and all reported city transport expenses at actual cost of RMB 180 based on receipts. Prof. Cheng, an academician, and Prof. Dai, classified as bureau-director-level and equivalent-duty personnel, both stayed under the bureau-director accommodation standard at an actual rate of RMB 520 per night. Prof. Xie, a deputy senior-title and above professional-technical personnel, filed under the same standard but the actual lodging rate written on the form was RMB 2,000 per night. The submission included intercity transport invoices, lodging invoices, and city transport receipts; Prof. Cheng and Prof. Xie each attached a "Fudan University Business Trip Report Form," while Prof. Dai's was not included; materials bearing the unit head's signature and the department (first-level budget unit) official seal were also included. None of the submissions included the approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting documents required when transport invoices cannot be obtained in remote research areas, nor the approved special circumstances explanation and handwritten receipt signed by the payee required when lodging invoices cannot be obtained. There were no instances of intercity transport or lodging expenses being covered by an external unit, nor any cases of transport or meal subsidies already paid by an off-campus unit being claimed again at the university.

case_ch: In 2026, the school continued to combine the overseas expense review for three teachers upon their return in the same batch. Prof. Jiang, Prof. Shao, and Prof. Liang all filed under non-research project funds, listing conference registration fees, living allowance, incidental expenses, and transportation costs on the overseas expense statement; boarding pass, accommodation receipts, visa fee receipts, and insurance fee receipts were all organized in the same round of materials; all expenses were reimbursed together after return, with foreign currency already selected in each appointment and automatically converted by the system; the materials had been reviewed and signed by the Foreign Affairs Office's Travel Center. Prof. Jiang, corresponding to bureau-director-level and above management personnel, did not submit specific transport mode receipts; the original or photocopy of the "Approval Document for Overseas Travel or Travel to Hong Kong and Macao" was attached. Prof. Shao, corresponding to bureau-director-level personnel, reported transport modes including economy-class airfare, hard-seat train, second-class seat on high-speed rail or EMU, and third-class ship cabin; however, no original or photocopy of the "Approval Document for Overseas Travel or Travel to Hong Kong and Macao" was included. Prof. Liang, corresponding to division-level and lower personnel, also reported transport modes including economy-class airfare, hard-seat train, second-class seat on high-speed rail or EMU, and third-class ship cabin, and the original or photocopy of the "Approval Document for Overseas Travel or Travel to Hong Kong and Macao" was also attached. None of the three submissions involved overseas purchases of books or materials, nor any prepayments for future travel.

case_ch: In June 2026, the school continued to organize three consecutive training sessions as part of the same round of capability-enhancement programs. The session managed by Prof. Ding was for bureau-director-level personnel, lasting 5 days, with accommodation expenses of RMB 2,000, meals of RMB 750, venue/materials/transport costs totaling RMB 350, and other expenses of RMB 150, totaling RMB 3,250; an out-of-town instructor was engaged, incurring lecture fees of RMB 1,000 plus lodging of RMB 400, meals of RMB 150, and transport of RMB 300; written approval was included for items exceeding the standard rate, but the "Fudan University Detailed List of Training Expense Expenditures" was not included for this session. The session managed by Prof. Pan was for division-level and lower personnel, also lasting 5 days, with accommodation of RMB 1,700, meals of RMB 650, venue/materials/transport of RMB 250, and other expenses of RMB 150, totaling RMB 2,750; the out-of-town instructor's fees were RMB 1,000 for lecture fees, RMB 340 for lodging, RMB 130 for meals, and RMB 300 for transport. The session managed by Prof. Li was for principal investigators of major national research projects, also lasting 5 days, with accommodation of RMB 2,500, meals of RMB 750, venue/materials/transport of RMB 400, and other expenses of RMB 230, totaling RMB 3,880; the out-of-town instructor's fees were RMB 1,000 for lecture fees, RMB 500 for lodging, RMB 150 for meals, and RMB 300 for transport. The administrator compiled the trainee list with contact information and the itemized expense receipts provided by the training service provider into all materials; the sessions managed by Prof. Pan and Prof. Li also included the "Fudan University Detailed List of Training Expense Expenditures;" written approvals were attached for all items exceeding the standard rate.

case_ch: In September 2026, the school processed the labor service fees of three experts who had participated in an academic week in the same batch. Prof. Wu, corresponding to division-level and lower personnel, completed 4 class hours of lecturing and 2 days of in-person consultation; the lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at RMB 450 per class hour, and the consultation fee was RMB 450 per day. Prof. Gao, a principal investigator of a major national research project, arrived at the school in the same batch and likewise completed 4 class hours of lecturing and 2 days of in-person consultation; the lecture fees were also declared on an after-tax basis at RMB 1,200 per class hour, and the consultation fee was RMB 1,000 per day. Prof. Jiang, a national-level second-tier professional-technical post personnel, also delivered 4 class hours of lecturing and participated in 2 days of in-person consultation, with a lecturing rate of RMB 1,200 per class hour and a consultation fee of RMB 700 per day, but the lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis. The entire batch of documents covered only lecture fees and expert consulting fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group submitted the travel expenses of three colleagues who had traveled to Beijing to advance their research project together for write-off. Prof. Zhao, Prof. Sun, and Prof. Qian all charged expenses to research project funds, none of the trips were made by the unit head personally, all traveled by first-class train seating (round trip), each stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of accommodation, all claimed a meal subsidy of RMB 300, and all reported city transport expenses at actual cost of RMB 180 based on receipts. Prof. Sun, a principal investigator of a major national research project, and Prof. Qian, an academician, both stayed under the bureau-director accommodation standard at an actual rate of RMB 520 per night. Prof. Zhao, classified as other personnel, filed under the same standard but the actual lodging rate written on the form was RMB 2,000 per night. The submission included intercity transport invoices, lodging invoices, and city transport receipts; Prof. Zhao and Prof. Qian each attached a "Fudan University Business Trip Report Form," while Prof. Sun's was not included; materials bearing the unit head's signature and the department (first-level budget unit) official seal were also included. None of the submissions included the approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting documents required when transport invoices cannot be obtained in remote research areas, nor the approved special circumstances explanation and handwritten receipt signed by the payee required when lodging invoices cannot be obtained. There were no instances of intercity transport or lodging expenses being covered by an external unit, nor any cases of transport or meal subsidies already paid by an off-campus unit being claimed again at the university.

case_ch: In 2026, the school arranged the overseas expense review for three scholars upon their return in the same batch. Prof. Chen, Prof. Tang, and Prof. Han all filed under non-research project funds, listing conference registration fees, living allowance, incidental expenses, and transportation costs on the overseas expense statement; boarding pass, accommodation receipts, visa fee receipts, and insurance fee receipts were all organized in the same round of materials; all expenses were reimbursed together after return, with foreign currency already selected in each appointment and automatically converted by the system; the materials had been reviewed and signed by the Foreign Affairs Office's Travel Center. Prof. Chen, a national-level second-tier professional-technical post personnel, reported transport modes including economy-class airfare, hard-seat train, second-class seat on high-speed rail or EMU, and third-class ship cabin; however, no original or photocopy of the "Approval Document for Overseas Travel or Travel to Hong Kong and Macao" was included for Prof. Chen. Prof. Tang, an academician, likewise reported transport modes including economy-class airfare, hard-seat train, second-class seat on high-speed rail or EMU, and third-class ship cabin, and the original or photocopy of the "Approval Document for Overseas Travel or Travel to Hong Kong and Macao" was also attached. Prof. Han, corresponding to bureau-director-level and above management personnel, did not submit specific transport mode receipts but also attached the original or photocopy of the "Approval Document for Overseas Travel or Travel to Hong Kong and Macao." None of the three submissions involved overseas purchases of books or materials, nor any prepayments for future travel.

case_ch: In July 2026, the university continued to bundle and submit reimbursement documents for three high-level training sessions. The training coordinated by Prof. Cheng was for academician-level participants; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, totaling 3,800 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with additional charges of 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Xie was for national senior management officials at senior deputy-ministerial level or above; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The training coordinated by Prof. Dai was for bureau-director-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,000 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 350 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,250 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 400 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized expense receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Cheng and Prof. Xie also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, after a college forum concluded, the labor service fees for three experts — management-track and teaching-track specialists — were processed together in the same batch. Prof. Zhu, classified as a national senior management official at senior deputy-ministerial level or above, completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour, and consultation fees were paid at 1,000 yuan per day. Prof. Huo, classified as a bureau-director-level official, also delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation, but lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis; the lecture rate was 900 yuan per class hour and the consultation rate was 700 yuan per day. The third expert, Prof. Shen, classified as a division-chief-level official or below, declared lecture fees on an after-tax basis at 450 yuan per class hour, with consultation fees at 450 yuan per day. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group submitted travel reimbursement documents together for three faculty members' trip to Beijing to advance phased project work. Prof. Ding, Prof. Pan, and Prof. Li all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Ding and Prof. Pan were classified as bureau-level officials (or equivalent duty) and post-salary-scale-5-or-above professional and technical personnel, respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Li was classified as other personnel; lodging was filed at the same bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Pan and Prof. Li also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included as well. None of the documents included an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for obtaining transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging expenses were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college continued to review overseas travel expenses for three faculty members upon their return to China. Prof. Zhou, Prof. Lin, and Prof. Wan all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all organized in the same round of documents; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Lin's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Zhou and Prof. Wan traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Zhou and Prof. Lin submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In June 2026, the college organized three consecutive high-level training sessions, with the same administrator handling the reimbursement in sequence. The training coordinated by Prof. Zhao was for leaders of major national research projects; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, totaling 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with additional charges of 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Sun was for national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan plus 500 yuan accommodation, 150 yuan meals, and 300 yuan transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Qian was for academician-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan plus 500 yuan accommodation, 150 yuan meals, and 300 yuan transportation. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized expense receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Zhao and Prof. Qian also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List — the one for Prof. Sun's session was not included; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, the college settled the labor service fees for three high-level experts in the same batch after an exchange week. Prof. Zhang, classified as a national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel, both delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis, with lecturing at 1,200 yuan per class hour and on-site consultation at 700 yuan per day. Academician Liang, arriving in the same cohort, also completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation at the same rates of 1,200 yuan per class hour and 700 yuan per consultation day, but with lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis. The third expert, Prof. Yan, classified as a national senior management official at senior deputy-ministerial level or above, likewise completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation, with lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis and consultation fees at 1,000 yuan per day. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group continued to submit travel reimbursement documents for three faculty members' trip to Beijing to advance the phased tasks. Prof. Cheng, Prof. Xie, and Prof. Dai all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Cheng and Prof. Dai were classified as an academician and a bureau-level official (or equivalent duty), respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Xie was classified as associate-senior-rank-or-above professional and technical personnel; lodging was filed at the same bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Cheng and Prof. Xie also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included as well. None of the documents contained an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging expenses were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college continued to review overseas travel expenses for three faculty members upon their return to China. Prof. Jiang, Prof. Shao, and Prof. Liang all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all organized in the same round of documents; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Jiang's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Shao and Prof. Liang traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Jiang and Prof. Liang submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In June 2026, the college organized three capacity-building training sessions in the same round and continued to process them together. The training coordinated by Prof. Ding was for bureau-director-level participants; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,000 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 350 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, totaling 3,250 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with additional charges of 400 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Pan was for division-chief-level participants and below; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 1,700 yuan, meals of 650 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 250 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 2,750 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 340 yuan for accommodation, 130 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Li was for leaders of major national research projects; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized expense receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Pan and Prof. Li also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, the college processed the labor service fees for three experts who attended an academic week in the same batch. Prof. Bao, classified as a division-chief-level official or below, completed 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at 450 yuan per class hour, and consultation fees were paid at 450 yuan per day. Prof. Sheng, a leader of a major national research project arriving in the same cohort, also completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour, and consultation fees were at 1,000 yuan per day. The third expert, Prof. Tan, classified as a national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel, likewise delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation, at a lecture rate of 1,200 yuan per class hour and a consultation rate of 700 yuan per day, but lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no doctoral or master's thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group submitted travel reimbursement documents together for three colleagues' trip to Beijing to advance the project. Prof. Zhao, Prof. Sun, and Prof. Qian all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Sun and Prof. Qian were classified as a leader of a major national research project and an academician, respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Zhao was classified as other personnel; lodging was filed at the bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Zhao and Prof. Qian also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included as well. None of the documents included an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging expenses were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college arranged to review overseas travel expenses for three scholars upon their return to China in the same round. Prof. Chen, Prof. Tang, and Prof. Han all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all organized in the same round of materials; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Han's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Chen and Prof. Tang traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Tang and Prof. Han submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In July 2026, the university compiled and submitted reimbursement materials for three high-level training sessions in a single batch. The session coordinated by Prof. Cheng was for academician-level participants; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, totaling 3,800 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The session coordinated by Prof. Xie was for national senior management officials at senior deputy-ministerial level and above; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The session coordinated by Prof. Dai was for bureau-director-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,000 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 350 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,250 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 400 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The administrator organized supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Cheng and Prof. Xie also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, after a college forum concluded, the labor service fees for three management-track and teaching-track expert specialists were processed together in the same batch. Prof. Qiu, classified as a national senior management official at senior deputy-ministerial level or above, completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour, and consultation fees were paid at 1,000 yuan per day. Prof. Jia, classified as a bureau-director-level official, also delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation, but lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis; the lecture rate was 900 yuan per class hour and the consultation rate was 700 yuan per day. The third expert, Prof. Fu, classified as a division-chief-level official or below, declared lecture fees on an after-tax basis at 450 yuan per class hour, with consultation fees at 450 yuan per day. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group continued to submit travel reimbursement documents for three faculty members on a phased work trip to Beijing. Prof. Ding, Prof. Pan, and Prof. Li all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Ding and Prof. Pan were classified as bureau-level officials (or equivalent duty) and post-salary-scale-5-or-above professional and technical personnel, respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Li was classified as other personnel; lodging was filed at the same bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Pan and Prof. Li also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included. None of the documents contained an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging expenses were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college continued to review overseas travel expenses for three faculty members upon their return to China. Prof. Zhou, Prof. Lin, and Prof. Wan all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all organized in the same round of materials; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Lin's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Zhou and Prof. Wan traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Zhou and Prof. Lin submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions, while Prof. Wan's submission did not include such approval documentation. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In June 2026, the college again organized three consecutive high-level training sessions, all arranged by the same administrator in sequence. The training coordinated by Prof. Zhao was for leaders of major national research projects; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, totaling 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Sun was for national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan plus 500 yuan accommodation, 150 yuan meals, and 300 yuan transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Qian was for academician-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan plus 500 yuan accommodation, 150 yuan meals, and 300 yuan transportation. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Zhao and Prof. Qian also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List, while Prof. Sun's session did not; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, the college settled the labor service fees for three high-level experts in the same batch after an exchange week. Prof. Ni, classified as a national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel, both delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis, with lecturing at 1,200 yuan per class hour and on-site consultation at 700 yuan per day. Academician Yu, arriving in the same cohort, also completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation at the same rates of 1,200 yuan per class hour and 700 yuan per consultation day, but with lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis. The third expert, Prof. Tao, classified as a national senior management official at senior deputy-ministerial level or above, likewise completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation, with lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis and consultation fees at 1,000 yuan per day. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group continued to consolidate and submit travel reimbursement documents for three faculty members' trip to Beijing to advance phased work. Prof. Cheng, Prof. Xie, and Prof. Dai all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Cheng and Prof. Dai were classified as an academician and a bureau-level official (or equivalent duty), respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Xie was classified as associate-senior-rank-or-above professional and technical personnel; lodging was filed at the same bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Cheng and Prof. Xie also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was also included. None of the documents contained an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging costs were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college reviewed overseas travel expenses for three faculty members upon their return to China in the same batch. Prof. Jiang, Prof. Shao, and Prof. Liang all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all compiled in the same round of documents; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Jiang's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Shao and Prof. Liang traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Jiang and Prof. Liang also submitted the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions along with their documents. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In June 2026, the college organized three capacity-building training sessions in the same round and continued to process them together. The training coordinated by Prof. Ding was for bureau-director-level participants; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,000 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 350 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, totaling 3,250 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with additional charges of 400 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Pan was for division-chief-level participants and below; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 1,700 yuan, meals of 650 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 250 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 2,750 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 340 yuan for accommodation, 130 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Li was for leaders of major national research projects; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Pan and Prof. Li also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List, while Prof. Ding's session did not; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, the college processed the labor service fees for three experts who attended an academic week in the same batch. Prof. Bao, classified as a division-chief-level official or below, completed 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at 450 yuan per class hour, and consultation fees were paid at 450 yuan per day. Prof. Sheng, a leader of a major national research project arriving in the same cohort, also completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour, and consultation fees were at 1,000 yuan per day. The third expert, Prof. Tan, classified as a national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel, likewise delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation at a lecture rate of 1,200 yuan per class hour and a consultation rate of 700 yuan per day, but lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no doctoral or master's thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group submitted travel reimbursement documents together for three colleagues' trip to Beijing to advance the project tasks. Prof. Zhao, Prof. Sun, and Prof. Qian all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Sun and Prof. Qian were classified as a leader of a major national research project and an academician, respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Zhao was classified as other personnel; lodging was filed at the bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Zhao and Prof. Qian also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included as well. None of the documents included an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging expenses were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college arranged to review overseas travel expenses for three scholars upon their return to China in the same round of materials. Prof. Chen, Prof. Tang, and Prof. Han all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all organized in the same round; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Han's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Chen and Prof. Tang traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Tang and Prof. Han submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In July 2026, the university continued to bundle and submit reimbursement documents for three high-level training sessions in a single batch. The training coordinated by Prof. Cheng was for academician-level participants; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, totaling 3,800 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The training coordinated by Prof. Xie was for national senior management officials at senior deputy-ministerial level and above; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The training coordinated by Prof. Dai was for bureau-director-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,000 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 350 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,250 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 400 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and transportation at 300 yuan. The administrator gathered supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Cheng and Prof. Xie also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, after a college forum concluded, the labor service fees for three management-track and teaching-track expert specialists were processed in the same batch. Prof. Qiu, classified as a national senior management official at senior deputy-ministerial level or above, completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour, and consultation fees were paid at 1,000 yuan per day. Prof. Jia, classified as a bureau-director-level official, also delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation, but lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis; the lecture rate was 900 yuan per class hour and the consultation rate was 700 yuan per day. The third expert, Prof. Fu, classified as a division-chief-level official or below, declared lecture fees on an after-tax basis at 450 yuan per class hour, with consultation fees at 450 yuan per day. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group continued to submit travel reimbursement documents for three faculty members on a staged work trip to Beijing. Prof. Ding, Prof. Pan, and Prof. Li all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Ding and Prof. Pan were classified as bureau-level officials (or equivalent duty) and post-salary-scale-5-or-above professional and technical personnel, respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Li was classified as other personnel; lodging was filed at the same bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Pan and Prof. Li also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included. None of the documents contained an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging expenses were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college continued to consolidate overseas travel expenses for three faculty members upon their return to China. Prof. Zhou, Prof. Lin, and Prof. Wan all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all organized in the same round of documents; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Lin's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Zhou and Prof. Wan traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Zhou and Prof. Lin submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In June 2026, the college organized another three consecutive high-level training sessions, processed by the same administrator in sequence. The training coordinated by Prof. Zhao was for leaders of major national research projects; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, totaling 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Sun was for national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan plus 500 yuan accommodation, 150 yuan meals, and 300 yuan transportation. The training coordinated by Prof. Qian was for academician-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan plus 500 yuan accommodation, 150 yuan meals, and 300 yuan transportation. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Zhao and Prof. Qian also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List, while Prof. Sun's session did not; all expenses exceeding the standard quota had written approval attached.

case_ch: In September 2026, the college settled the labor service fees for three high-level experts in the same batch after an exchange week. Prof. Leng, classified as a national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel, both delivered 4 class hours of lectures and participated in 2 days of on-site consultation; lecture fees were not declared on an after-tax basis, with lecturing at 1,200 yuan per class hour and on-site consultation at 700 yuan per day. Academician Xing, arriving in the same cohort, also completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation at the same rates of 1,200 yuan per class hour and 700 yuan per consultation day, but with lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis. The third expert, Prof. Luo, classified as a national senior management official at senior deputy-ministerial level or above, likewise completed 4 class hours of lectures and 2 days of on-site consultation, with lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis and consultation fees at 1,000 yuan per day. The entire batch covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the research group continued to consolidate and submit travel reimbursement documents for three faculty members' trip to Beijing to advance the phased work plan. Prof. Cheng, Prof. Xie, and Prof. Dai all charged expenses to the research fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All three traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Cheng and Prof. Dai were classified as an academician and a bureau-level official (or equivalent duty), respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Xie was classified as associate-senior-rank-or-above professional and technical personnel; lodging was filed at the same bureau-level standard, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Cheng and Prof. Xie also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included. None of the documents contained an approved special circumstances explanation or other valid supporting materials for transportation tickets in remote research areas, nor the approved statement or handwritten payee receipt for situations where lodging invoices cannot be obtained. No intercity transportation or lodging costs were covered by external units, and there was no duplicate claiming of transportation or meal expenses already paid by an external unit.

case_ch: In 2026, the college reviewed overseas travel expenses for three faculty members upon their return to China in the same batch of materials. Prof. Jiang, Prof. Shao, and Prof. Liang all reported conference registration fees, overseas living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation costs under the non-research fund category; airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts were all compiled in the same round of documents; all expenses were reimbursed together upon return, with foreign currency selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Jiang's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Shao and Prof. Liang traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. The batch was reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center; Prof. Jiang and Prof. Liang submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions. None of the three had overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or expenses prepaid for future travel.

case_ch: In May 2026, the Humanities Computing Laboratory submitted travel reimbursement documents for all accompanying faculty members together when they traveled to Beijing for a themed document review session. Prof. Shen, Prof. Gu, Prof. Zhao, and Prof. Sun all charged expenses to the research project fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All four traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Shen, Prof. Zhao, and Prof. Sun were classified as an academician, bureau-level officials (or equivalent duty), and post-salary-scale-5-or-above professional and technical personnel, respectively; all three stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Gu was classified as associate-senior-rank-or-above professional and technical personnel; lodging was filed at the bureau-level standard as well, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Shen, Prof. Gu, and Prof. Sun also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included. The documents contained no references to transportation ticket certificates for remote research areas, lodging payment receipts, expenses covered by other units, or duplicate claims for transport or meal subsidies already paid by external organizations.

case_ch: In 2026, the Cognitive Science Platform consolidated overseas travel expenses for several faculty members upon their return to China in a single round of materials. Prof. Sun, Prof. Qian, Prof. Feng, and Prof. Wu all reported expenses under the non-research project fund category, with overseas expense statements covering conference registration fees, living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation. All four reimbursed expenses upon returning to China, with international airfare and accommodation paid by bank transfer; foreign currency was selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Sun and Prof. Wu's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Qian and Prof. Feng traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. Supporting documents included overseas expense statements, airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts; Prof. Sun, Prof. Feng, and Prof. Wu submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions, and the batch was then reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center. The documents contained no overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or prepaid expenses for future travel.

case_ch: In 2026, the Excellent Teacher Development Center grouped several consecutively scheduled thematic training sessions on 'Methodology Training' into a single round of training expense documents for joint submission. The session coordinated by Prof. Wu was primarily for bureau-director-level participants; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,000 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 350 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, totaling 3,250 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 400 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Chen was primarily for division-chief-level participants and below; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 1,700 yuan, meals of 650 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 250 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 2,750 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 340 yuan for accommodation, 130 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for intercity transportation, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Tang was primarily for leaders of major national research projects; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for intercity transportation, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Han was primarily for national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with 500 yuan for accommodation, 150 yuan for meals, and 300 yuan for intercity transportation, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Chen and Prof. Tang also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List.

case_ch: In 2026, the Interdisciplinary Innovation Platform compiled the labor service fees for several academic activities from the 'Interdisciplinary Research Forum' into a single round of materials. All activities coordinated by Prof. Zhao included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the division-chief-level-and-below standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis at 450 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 450 yuan per person-day. All activities coordinated by Prof. Sun included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the leader-of-major-national-research-project standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 1,000 yuan per person-day. All activities coordinated by Prof. Qian included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on a non-after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 700 yuan per person-day. All activities coordinated by Prof. Feng included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the academician standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 700 yuan per person-day. The entire round of materials covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the Fundamental Medicine Platform submitted travel reimbursement documents for all accompanying faculty members together when they traveled to Beijing for a joint research synthesis meeting. Prof. Cheng, Prof. Xie, Prof. Dai, and Prof. Zheng all charged expenses to the research project fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All four traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Xie and Prof. Dai were classified as a leader of a major national research project and an academician, respectively; both stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Cheng and Prof. Zheng were classified as other personnel and associate-senior-rank-or-above professional and technical personnel, respectively; lodging for both was filed at the bureau-level standard as well, but the actual prices recorded were 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Cheng, Prof. Dai, and Prof. Zheng also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included. The documents contained no references to transportation ticket certificates for remote research areas, lodging payment receipts, expenses covered by other units, or duplicate claims for transport or meal subsidies already paid by external organizations.

case_ch: In 2026, the Public Health Research Group consolidated overseas travel expenses for several faculty members upon their return to China in a single round of materials. Prof. Zheng, Prof. Xu, Prof. Jiang, and Prof. Shao all reported expenses under the non-research project fund category, with overseas expense statements covering conference registration fees, living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation. All four reimbursed expenses upon returning to China, with international airfare and accommodation paid by bank transfer; foreign currency was selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Jiang's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Zheng, Prof. Xu, and Prof. Shao traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. Supporting documents included overseas expense statements, airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts; Prof. Xu and Prof. Jiang submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions, and the batch was then reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center. The documents contained no overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or prepaid expenses for future travel.

case_ch: In 2026, the Youth Scholars Academy grouped several consecutively scheduled thematic training sessions on 'Specialized Capacity Building' into a single round of training expense documents for joint submission. The session coordinated by Prof. Shao was primarily for academician-level participants; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, totaling 3,800 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Liang was primarily for national senior management officials at senior deputy-ministerial level and above; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Cao was primarily for bureau-director-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,000 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 350 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,250 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 400 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Song was primarily for division-chief-level participants and below; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 1,700 yuan, meals of 650 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 250 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 2,750 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 340 yuan, meals at 130 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Shao, Prof. Liang, and Prof. Song also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List.

case_ch: In 2026, the Frontier Forum Secretariat compiled the labor service fees for several academic activities from the 'Project Demonstration Forum' into a single round of materials. All activities coordinated by Prof. Wu included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the national senior management official at senior deputy-ministerial level or above standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 1,000 yuan per person-day. All activities coordinated by Prof. Chen included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the bureau-director-level standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on a non-after-tax basis at 900 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 700 yuan per person-day. All activities coordinated by Prof. Tang included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the division-chief-level-and-below standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis at 450 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 450 yuan per person-day. All activities coordinated by Prof. Han included both lecture fees and expert consultation fees; the speaker was classified under the leader-of-major-national-research-project standard, with 4 class hours equivalent to 1 half-day, lecture fees declared on an after-tax basis at 1,200 yuan per class hour; consultation participants were classified under the same standard, with 2 consecutive days of on-site consultation at 1,000 yuan per person-day. The entire round of materials covered only lecture fees and expert consultation fees, with no graduate thesis review or defense-related expenses.

case_ch: In May 2026, the Public Health Research Group submitted travel reimbursement documents for all accompanying faculty members together when they traveled to Beijing for a centralized data review. Prof. Li, Prof. Lu, Prof. Peng, and Prof. Zhou all charged expenses to the research project fund; none was the unit head traveling on personal business. All four traveled by train first class and stayed in Beijing for 3 days with 2 nights of lodging, each claiming a meal subsidy of 300 yuan and local transportation reimbursed by receipts for 180 yuan. Prof. Li, Prof. Lu, and Prof. Zhou were classified as bureau-level officials (or equivalent duty), post-salary-scale-5-or-above professional and technical personnel, and a leader of a major national research project, respectively; all three stayed at the bureau-level lodging quota with actual lodging at 520 yuan per night. Prof. Peng was classified as other personnel; lodging was filed at the bureau-level standard as well, but the actual price recorded was 2,000 yuan per night. Supporting documents included intercity transportation invoices, lodging invoices, and local transportation receipts; Prof. Lu and Prof. Peng also attached a Fudan University Business Trip Report Form, and the unit head's signed and department-sealed (primary budget unit) documentation was included. The documents contained no references to transportation ticket certificates for remote research areas, lodging payment receipts, expenses covered by other units, or duplicate claims for transport or meal subsidies already paid by external organizations.

case_ch: In 2026, the International Cooperation Office consolidated overseas travel expenses for several faculty members upon their return to China in a single round of materials. Prof. Zhou, Prof. Lin, Prof. Wan, and Prof. Shen all reported expenses under the non-research project fund category, with overseas expense statements covering conference registration fees, living allowances, personal incidental expenses, and intercity transportation. All four reimbursed expenses upon returning to China, with international airfare and accommodation paid by bank transfer; foreign currency was selected at the time of appointment and automatically converted by the system. Prof. Lin's transport class was submitted at the applicable standard. Prof. Zhou, Prof. Wan, and Prof. Shen traveled by economy class air, hard-seat train, second-class high-speed rail, and third-class ship. Supporting documents included overseas expense statements, airfare boarding passes, accommodation receipts, visa fees, and insurance receipts; Prof. Zhou, Prof. Lin, and Prof. Shen submitted their documents along with the original or copy of the Approval Document for Overseas and Hong Kong/Macao Missions, and the batch was then reviewed and signed by the Office of International Affairs' outbound travel service center. The documents contained no overseas book purchases, overseas material purchases, or prepaid expenses for future travel.

case_ch: In 2026, the Medical Data Workshop grouped several consecutively scheduled thematic training sessions on 'Project Management Practice' into a single round of training expense documents for joint submission. The session coordinated by Prof. Shen was primarily for leaders of major national research projects; each session ran for 5 days, with accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meal costs of 750 yuan, combined venue/materials/transportation costs of 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, totaling 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Gu was primarily for national-level second-tier professional and technical personnel; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Zhao was primarily for academician-level participants; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 150 yuan, for a total of 3,800 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The session coordinated by Prof. Sun was primarily for national senior management officials at senior deputy-ministerial level and above; the 5-day session had accommodation costs of 2,500 yuan, meals of 750 yuan, venue/materials/transport totaling 400 yuan, and other expenses of 230 yuan, for a total of 3,880 yuan; the out-of-town instructor's lecture fee was 1,000 yuan, with accommodation at 500 yuan, meals at 150 yuan, and intercity transportation at 300 yuan, with written approval for exceeding the standard attached. The administrator compiled supporting documents including a trainee roster with contact information and the itemized receipts from the training service provider; the sessions coordinated by Prof. Shen, Prof. Zhao, and Prof. Sun also included a Fudan University Training Expense Detailed List.
